preamble: The change from the voiceless dental fricative /θ/ to the palatal glide /j/ is a less common shift, often found in specific dialectal variations. It represents a significant jump in place of articulation from dental to palatal. This might be considered a form of extreme reduction or substitution, potentially influenced by analogy or contact with other languages.

examples.note: A possible, though non-standard, substitution pattern.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
